docs.title: 📉 Welcome to the Quant Competitions Documentation
docs.text: This site hosts the unified documentation for high-performance trading solutions developed for elite quantitative competitions.

docs.title: 🚀 Highlights
docs.text:
• Tri-Language Support: All solutions implemented in Python 3.13, C++26, and Rust 1.93.1.
  • Production-Grade: Scalable, tested, and optimized for low latency.
  • Unified Build: Built and verified using Bazel.

docs.title: 🏗️ Monorepo Structure
docs.text:
We organize our work by institution and competition: - Citadel - Jane Street - Two Sigma

Explore the Technical Specification for deeper architecture details.

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: The Quant Competitions Monorepo serves as a centralized, high-performance repository for automated trading systems and quantitative modeling solutions. It is designed to meet the rigorous standards of top-tier quantitative hedge funds (Citadel, Jane Street, HRT).

docs.title: 2. Architecture
docs.text: The monorepo utilizes a multi-language, unified build system (Bazel) to ensure reproducibility and consistency across diverse components.

docs.title: 2.1 Directory Structure
docs.text:
• citadel/: Projects related to Citadel competitions.
  • jane_street/: Projects related to Jane Street competitions.
  • two_sigma/: Projects related to Two Sigma competitions.
  • .github/workflows/: Automated CI/CD pipelines.
  • docs/: Unified documentation for all projects.
  • technical_specification/: Detailed requirements and design docs.

docs.title: 2.2 Core Tech Stack
docs.text:
• Languages:
  • Python 3.13 (for rapid prototyping and data science)
  • C++26 (for high-frequency execution)
  • Rust 1.93.1 (for memory-safe, high-performance systems)
  • Build System: Bazel 7.x
  • Infrastructure: Docker for containerization and environment isolation.

docs.title: 3.1 Performance
docs.text:
• All execution paths must be optimized for low latency.
  • C++ and Rust solutions must target zero-allocation in the main trading loop.
  • Python solutions must utilize vectorized operations (NumPy/Pandas) where appropriate.

docs.title: 3.2 Robustness
docs.text:
• Fault Tolerance: Bots must handle network drops and API timeouts gracefully.
  • State Persistence: Strategies should be able to recover their state after a restart.
  • Risk Management: Mandatory "Kill Switch" and position limits must be implemented.

docs.title: 3.3 Documentation
docs.text:
• Each project must have a README.md explaining:
  • Strategy logic.
  • Mathematical foundations.
  • Setup and execution instructions.
  • All code must be documented using Doxygen (C++), Rustdoc (Rust), or Sphinx (Python).

docs.title: 4.1 Citadel Trading Competition
docs.text:
• Goal: Implement a bot capable of Exchange Arbitrage, Index Arbitrage, and Shock Handling.
  • Input: Real-time order books, news feed, and ticker history.
  • Metrics: Net Liquidating Value (NLV) and Sharpe Ratio.

docs.title: 4.2 Jane Street Electronic Trading Challenge
docs.text:
• Goal: Market making on multiple instruments.
  • Key Strategy: Dynamic hedging and spread optimization.

docs.title: 5. CI/CD & Testing
docs.text:
• Unit Testing: Minimum 80% code coverage across all languages.
  • Integration Testing: End-to-end simulation of trading days.
  • Performance Benchmarking: Automated latency checks for critical paths in C++ and Rust.

docs.title: 6. Security
docs.text:
• Secrets (API Keys) must never be committed to source control.
  • Use .env files or system environment variables for sensitive configuration.

Last Updated: 2026-03-01

docs.title: 📋 Problem Description
docs.text:
The Citadel Trading Competition challenges participants to build a robust, high-performance automated trading bot capable of extracting value from simulated market inefficiencies.

The environment includes: - Multiple Exchanges: Identical securities (e.g., WMT-M, WMT-A) trading on different venues. - ETFs: Index funds (e.g., ETF) whose price should reflect the sum of its underlying components (WMT, CAT, MMM). - News Feed: Real-time shocks that impact prices predictably over a short window.

docs.title: Key Objectives
docs.text:
1. Minimize Latency: Fast response to price crosses and news.
  2. Maximize NLV: Net Liquidating Value through arbitrage and directional news plays.
  3. Risk Management: Staying within gross and net position limits.

docs.title: 🚀 Solutions Overview
docs.text: Solution Language Key Technologies Python 3.13 Python Poetry, Pydantic v2, HTTPX C++26 C++ CMake, CLI11, CPR, nlohmann/json Rust 1.93.1 Rust Cargo, Reqwest, Serde, Clap

docs.title: 🐍 Python 3.13 Solution Detail
docs.text: Implemented using modern Python 3.13 features like type statement for type aliases and improved generics.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: The bot follows a clean Strategy Pattern. The TradingBot orchestrator polls for new ticks and delegates execution to ExchangeArbitrage, IndexArbitrage, and ShockHandler.

docs.title: Performance Optimization
docs.text:
• Vectorized Logic: Uses efficient list comprehensions and generator expressions for data processing.
  • Async Polling: Leverages high-performance HTTPX client.

docs.title: ⚙️ C++26 Solution Detail
docs.text: A high-performance implementation utilizing C++26 features for maximum efficiency.

docs.title: Modern C++26 Features Used:
docs.text:
• std::println: For zero-overhead, type-safe logging.
  • Pack Indexing: Simplified template metaprogramming for strategy parameter handling.
  • constexpr Improvements: More logic moved to compile-time to reduce runtime latency.
  • Placeholder variables: Using _ for unused structured binding results.

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Zero-Allocation Loop: The main trading loop is designed to avoid heap allocations.
  • Cache-Friendly: Data structures are optimized for CPU cache locality.

docs.title: 🦀 Rust 1.93.1 Solution Detail
docs.text: Focuses on Memory Safety and Zero-Cost Abstractions.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: Uses Trait-based strategy definitions to ensure compile-time polymorphism without virtual dispatch overhead.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Strict Typing: Prevents common financial logic errors (e.g., mixing currencies or side types).
  • Concurrency: Safe multi-threaded polling and execution.
  • Error Handling: Comprehensive error propagation using anyhow.

docs.title: 📊 Strategy Comparison
docs.text: Strategy Logic Complexity Exchange Arb \(P_{Bid, A} - P_{Ask, B} >     ext{Fees}\) \(O(1)\) Index Arb $ P_{ETF} - \sum P_{Comp} Shock Handler Immediate position on news + Reversal \(O(1)\)

docs.title: 📋 Electronic Trading Challenge
docs.text: Participants must develop a market-making bot for a simulated electronic exchange.

docs.title: Problem Description
docs.text:
• Market Making: Providing liquidity by posting both buy and sell orders.
  • Fair Value Estimation: Calculating the true value of an asset based on order book signals.
  • Risk Management: Managing position limits and hedging market risk.

docs.title: Solutions
docs.text:
• Python 3.13
  • C++26
  • Rust 1.93.1

docs.title: 📈 Market Prediction
docs.text: A data science challenge focused on predicting future market movements from a set of anonymous features.

docs.title: Problem Description
docs.text:
• Time-Series Analysis: Handling temporal dependencies in market data.
  • Inference Latency: Models must be efficient enough for real-time application.
  • Evaluation: Scored on Utility score (weighted sum of returns).

docs.title: Solutions
docs.text:
• Python 3.13
  • C++26
  • Rust 1.93.1

docs.title: 📋 Financial Modeling Challenge
docs.text: Develop models to predict the future behavior of assets using a rich set of financial data.

docs.title: Problem Description
docs.text:
• Feature Engineering: Transforming raw financial data into predictive features.
  • Model Selection: Balancing model complexity with generalization.
  • Backtesting: Evaluating performance on historical data.

docs.title: Solutions
docs.text:
• Python 3.13
  • C++26
  • Rust 1.93.1

docs.title: 🗞️ Predicting Stock using News
docs.text: Using natural language processing to extract market signals from news sentiment.

docs.title: Problem Description
docs.text:
• Sentiment Analysis: Quantifying the impact of news on stock prices.
  • NLP Pipelines: Processing large volumes of news data in real-time.
  • Multi-Modal Data: Combining news sentiment with historical price data.

docs.title: Solutions
docs.text:
• Python 3.13
  • C++26
  • Rust 1.93.1
